title: Realty Income Declares 134th Consecutive Monthly Dividend Increase — Why Monthly Payers Are Surging
description: Realty Income (O) announced its 134th consecutive monthly dividend increase. Here's why monthly dividend stocks are surging in demand as markets get choppy.
lede: Realty Income Corporation (O) announced its 134th consecutive common stock monthly dividend increase on March 11. The REIT has paid dividends every month since 1969, making it a bellwether for income investors — and demand for monthly dividend stocks is accelerating as market volatility makes predictable cash flow more valuable than ever.

faqs.q: How many consecutive monthly dividend increases has Realty Income declared?
faqs.a: As of March 2026, Realty Income has declared 134 consecutive common stock monthly dividend increases, one of the longest active streaks among REITs.

faqs.q: What is Realty Income's current dividend yield?
faqs.a: Realty Income currently yields approximately 4.97%, paying $3.22 per share annually at a share price around $65.

faqs.q: What are the best monthly dividend stocks in 2026?
faqs.a: Top monthly dividend payers include Realty Income (O) at 4.97%, AGNC Investment (AGNC) at 13.5%, JEPI ETF at 7.9%, Main Street Capital (MAIN) at 5.35%, and Prospect Capital (PSEC) at 20.3%. Our list tracks 40+ monthly payers across REITs, BDCs, ETFs, and CEFs.

faqs.q: Why do investors prefer monthly dividends over quarterly?
faqs.a: Monthly dividends match real expenses like rent, groceries, and bills. They also compound faster when reinvested — 12 compounding events per year vs 4 — and provide a psychological benefit of seeing regular income.

faqs.q: Is Realty Income a good long-term investment?
faqs.a: Realty Income has delivered total returns above 13% annually since its 1994 IPO, with a $61B market cap and 15,000+ commercial properties. Its net-lease model with long-term tenant contracts provides stable cash flow.

sections.heading: What Happened
paragraphs: Realty Income declared a monthly dividend of $0.2685 per share for April 2026, representing a 2.1% year-over-year increase. The company has now raised its common stock dividend 134 months in a row — one of the longest active streaks among publicly traded REITs.

At a share price of roughly $65, that works out to a 4.97% yield — paying investors $3.22 annually, or about $26.85 per month for every 100 shares. The company owns over 15,000 commercial properties across the U.S. and Europe, with tenants like Walgreens, Dollar General, and FedEx locked into long-term net leases.

sections.heading: Why Monthly Dividend Stocks Are Surging in Demand
paragraphs: With the S&P 500 down sharply amid the Hormuz crisis and rising trade tensions, investors are rotating into income-producing assets. Monthly dividend stocks like Realty Income are seeing elevated inflows because they provide predictable cash flow regardless of share price movements.

The math is straightforward: a $100,000 portfolio split across 5-7 monthly payers at an average 8% yield generates roughly $667/month in passive income. That's real money hitting your account every 30 days — not every 90.

Use our Monthly Dividend Stocks list to calculate exact income for any investment amount — with an interactive income calculator, 40 stocks sorted by yield, and type filters for REITs, BDCs, ETFs, and CEFs.

sections.heading: The Monthly Dividend Landscape in 2026
paragraphs: Beyond Realty Income, the monthly dividend space has expanded significantly. Covered-call ETFs like JEPI (7.9% yield) and JEPQ (10.6%) now manage over $70 billion combined, offering monthly income from options premiums. BDCs like Main Street Capital (5.35%) and Ares Capital (8.8%) lend to middle-market companies and pass through monthly distributions.

At the higher end of the yield spectrum, closed-end funds like PIMCO Dynamic Income (PDI, 13.5%) and Oxford Lane Capital (OXLC, 18%) offer double-digit monthly payouts — though they come with higher risk, including NAV erosion and return-of-capital distributions that investors should understand before buying.
The key insight: monthly dividends aren't just for retirees anymore. Younger investors are building "paycheck replacement" portfolios designed to cover living expenses entirely from dividend income.

sections.heading: How to Evaluate Monthly Dividend Stocks
paragraphs: Not all high yields are sustainable. The biggest trap in monthly dividend investing is chasing yield without examining payout coverage. A REIT paying 15% but funding it from borrowed capital will eventually cut. Look for funds-from-operations (FFO) payout ratios below 85% for REITs, and net investment income (NII) coverage above 100% for BDCs and CEFs.

Diversification across types matters too. REITs provide real estate exposure, BDCs offer private credit, covered-call ETFs give equity market participation with income, and CEFs access bond and infrastructure strategies. Spreading across 2-3 types reduces single-sector risk.
